fast.summary: This paper benchmarks the two main families of approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS) methods — graph-based (HNSW, DiskANN) and partition-based (Faiss-IVF+PQ, ScaNN) — specifically for entity resolution tasks at million-vector scale, using a new semi-synthetic benchmark with ground-truth labels. It measures both retrieval speed and ER quality metrics (precision, recall, F1) to give practitioners clear guidance on which architecture to choose.
fast.key_idea: Graph-based ANNS methods dominate when high recall/F1 is required, while partition-based methods like ScaNN win on throughput when moderate recall is acceptable.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners building large-scale deduplication or record linkage pipelines must choose an ANNS backend; this paper provides the first ER-specific, apples-to-apples comparison with actionable trade-off guidance rather than generic ANN benchmarks.
possible_use_cases: Customer deduplication in CRM or MDM systems where matching quality (F1) is paramount, favoring HNSW or DiskANN
High-throughput product catalog matching in e-commerce where ScaNN's speed advantage at moderate recall is acceptable
Knowledge graph entity linking pipelines that need to balance indexing memory footprint (DiskANN's disk-based approach) against query latency at billion-record scale

deep.method_breakdown: The paper frames Entity Resolution as an Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search (ANNS) problem over high-dimensional embeddings, then systematically compares graph-based methods (HNSW, DiskANN) against partition-based methods (Faiss-IVF+PQ, ScaNN) on a new semi-synthetic benchmark of two one-million-vector datasets with known ground truth. Evaluation goes beyond standard ANNS metrics (recall@k, QPS) to include ER-specific metrics—precision, recall, F1—and disaggregates latency into blocking time (index traversal) and matching time (distance computation). This dual-lens evaluation reveals trade-offs invisible to purely retrieval-centric benchmarks.
deep.key_innovation: The primary innovation is a semi-synthetic ER-specific benchmark at one-million scale with known ground truth, enabling rigorous measurement of ER quality metrics (precision/recall/F1) rather than just ANNS recall@k. Prior ANNS benchmarks (ANN-Benchmarks, Big-ANN-Benchmarks) are retrieval-agnostic and do not capture the asymmetric cost structure of ER workloads. The disaggregated timing (blocking vs. matching) is also novel, exposing where each architecture's bottleneck lies under ER-specific query distributions.
deep.technical_insight: The most critical design choice is the architectural separation between index traversal (blocking) and candidate scoring (matching). Graph-based methods like HNSW maintain globally connected proximity graphs, so traversal is adaptive and recall scales gracefully with ef_search at the cost of memory bandwidth; this makes them dominant when F1 must be maximized regardless of throughput. Partition-based methods like ScaNN use quantization-compressed inverted lists, enabling aggressive batching and SIMD-level parallelism that yields far higher QPS at moderate recall, but recall plateaus sharply because quantization loss and IVF boundary effects are irreducible.
deep.limitations: The benchmark is semi-synthetic, so embedding distributions may not capture real-world ER noise patterns (OCR errors, transliterations, schema heterogeneity) that would shift the recall-precision frontier. Only four ANNS systems are evaluated, omitting recent hybrid methods (e.g., SPANN, NGT-PANNG, Milvus's HNSW+IVF hybrids). The study uses a single embedding model, so conclusions may not generalize across embedding dimensionalities or semantic spaces. Distributed/sharded deployment scenarios—common at production scale beyond one million vectors—are not addressed.
interview_questions: Why does HNSW outperform partition-based methods at high-recall regimes for ER, and what graph property drives this?
What is the fundamental reason ScaNN achieves higher throughput than HNSW at moderate recall, and what is the irreducible cost that caps its recall ceiling?
How does disaggregating blocking vs. matching time change the practitioner's decision compared to using total QPS alone?
What are the memory and disk I/O trade-offs between DiskANN and HNSW, and when does DiskANN's SSD-based design become preferable?
How would you extend this benchmark methodology to evaluate ANNS systems in a distributed ER pipeline where blocking must be parallelized across shards?
interview_answers: HNSW builds a hierarchical navigable small-world graph where nodes are connected to their approximate nearest neighbors at multiple granularity layers. During search, traversal starts at the coarse top layer and greedily descends, so the search path is globally informed and can escape local optima. This adaptive routing means recall improves continuously as ef_search increases with no hard ceiling, unlike inverted index boundaries that can structurally exclude true neighbors assigned to different partitions.

ScaNN uses anisotropic vector quantization (AVQ) and inverted file indexing to compress vectors into compact codes that fit in cache, enabling SIMD-parallelized batch dot-product scoring across thousands of candidates simultaneously. The throughput gain comes from cache efficiency and vectorized arithmetic. However, quantization introduces irreversible information loss, and IVF partitioning means true neighbors near Voronoi boundaries are only retrieved if their partition is probed—increasing nprobe helps but hits diminishing returns, creating a recall ceiling that graph methods do not share.

Total QPS conflates two fundamentally different costs: the index traversal (blocking) that determines which candidates are retrieved, and the re-scoring/matching step that computes final similarity. For ER, blocking latency dominates at high nprobe or large ef_search, while matching latency scales with candidate set size. A system with fast blocking but slow matching (e.g., exact re-scoring over many candidates) may look worse on total QPS but achieve better F1 per unit of blocking budget. Disaggregation lets practitioners tune each stage independently and identify the true bottleneck.

HNSW stores the entire graph in RAM, which becomes prohibitive beyond ~100M vectors on commodity hardware. DiskANN stores the graph on SSD and uses a beam-search strategy with prefetching to amortize SSD latency across parallel I/O requests, keeping only a small in-memory cache of compressed vectors for beam pruning. DiskANN is preferable when dataset size exceeds available RAM, latency SLAs tolerate ~1ms SSD access overhead, and cost constraints rule out high-memory instances. For latency-critical in-memory workloads, HNSW remains faster.

In a distributed ER pipeline, blocking must be parallelized by sharding the index across nodes, which introduces cross-shard boundary problems analogous to IVF partition boundary effects. The benchmark should be extended to measure shard-recall (fraction of true matches retrieved when each query only searches k shards) as a function of shard count and replication factor. One would evaluate routing strategies—hash-based, learned partition assignment, or broadcast-to-all—measuring total system QPS, end-to-end F1, and network communication cost, since graph-based methods require more replication to avoid boundary misses while partition-based methods map more naturally to distributed inverted indexes.
